original_abstract:
Three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have captivated the scientific community as a premier material for high-efficiency, low-cost solar energy conversion. However, despite their impressive performance, 3D perovskites suffer from significant environmental and operational instabilities. In parallel, two-dimensional (2D) halide perovskites have emerged as a chemically robust subclass, offering enhanced environmental resilience due to their layered structure and hydrophobic organic spacers, though they often lag in charge-transport efficiency. Combining these two materials into 3D–2D mixed-dimensional systems presents a powerful strategy for developing optoelectronic devices that synergize the superior efficiency of 3D phases with the exceptional durability of 2D architectures.

This review provides a comprehensive analysis of the structural and electronic differences between 3D and 2D perovskites, detailing how reduced dimensionality and quantum confinement dictate their fundamental material properties. We examine various integration strategies, from 2D cation doping within 3D matrices to the fabrication of sophisticated 3D/2D heterostructure stacks. Special attention is given to recent processing breakthroughs that allow for precise control over phase purity, crystal orientation, and interface sharpness. Beyond photovoltaics, we explore the potential of these synergistic systems in light-emitting diodes, photodetectors, and lasing applications. Finally, we offer a perspective on the future research directions needed to realize mixed-dimensional perovskite technologies that meet the rigorous stability and performance requirements for commercial deployment.
humanized_abstract:
In the last decade, three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have become some of the most popular materials for efficient solar energy conversion, because of their remarkable combination of high power conversion efficiency (PCE) and low cost. However, 3D perovskites still face significant challenges related to environmental stability and operational durability. In contrast, two-dimensional (2D) halide perovskites present a chemically stable subclass of perovskites materials, exhibiting greatly enhanced stability in relation to ambient environment, because of the steric shielding afforded by the layered structure of these compounds, as well as by the hydrophobic nature of the organic spacers located in between. Moreover, 2D perovskites are typically characterized by low charge carrier mobility, hence not matching the efficiency potential that is instead achieved by their 3D counterparts. Combining 3D structures with 2D domains in 3D–2D mixed-dimensional lead halide perovskites could thus pave the way for the realization of solar energy devices that keep the excellent photovoltaic performance of 3D perovskites, while achieving significantly improved operational stability, like the one already demonstrated by their 2D counterparts.

This review summarizes the structural and electronic differences between 3D and 2D perovskites, and elucidates how the 3D to 2D reduction and corresponding quantum confinement impacts their material properties. We discuss the strategies of incorporating 2D cation dopants into 3D frameworks and developing 3D/2D heterostructures. We highlight very recent breakthroughs in preparing mixed-dimensional perovskites with desired phase purity, crystal plane orientation and interface sharpness. Beyond photovoltaics, we also discuss the potential of MDPSs in LEDs, detectors and lasers. Finally, we give our point of view on the needed research in the future to reach the required stability and efficiency for the commercialization of MDPSs.
